data.title: US needs ‘better understanding of China’ to shift the dial, former diplomat says
data.description:
The tone was “constructive” at the US-China leaders’ summit but the two powers remain at odds on key issues – and that will not change unless Washington changes its approach, a former American diplomat said.
David Firestein, inaugural president and CEO of the George H.W. Bush Foundation for US-China Relations, said American policy must be grounded on a better understanding of China.
And he said Washington needed “smarter policies that work better for the American people”.
In a speech at the Centre on Contemporary China and the World at the University of Hong Kong on Wednesday, Firestein said the fundamentals of the relationship would not change because of “profound and irreconcilable differences of view” on issues ranging from Taiwan and the South China Sea to human rights.
“It’s just not going to happen,” Firestein said. “It doesn’t matter how many presidential summits we have, or who’s in the White House, or who the leader of China is at some point in the future.”

As a diplomat, Firestein worked across four US administrations from 1992 to 2010 with a focus on Washington-Beijing relations.
Firestein noted the “constructive tone” of the meeting between Donald Trump and Xi Jinping in Busan, South Korea on October 30.
But he said it could fall back to the pattern of the last nine years since Trump’s first term in office that began in 2017.
“[The Trump administration] created friction and created a trade war tonality, and then, a little while later, we’ll undo some of that, and then declare a victory,” Firestein said, citing the agreements reached between Xi and Trump two weeks ago.
Their meeting resulted in a temporary truce in the trade war. China suspended its wide export ban on critical minerals and committed to resuming large-scale purchases of US soybeans and other agricultural products. The US meanwhile agreed to halve its fentanyl-related tariffs on Chinese goods to 10 per cent and both sides agreed to a one-year suspension of the special port fees imposed on each other’s vessels.
But Firestein said a change in tone would not be enough to improve ties, and a change in perception and policies was needed.
“The substance is still not in a good place,” he said. “It’s not going to be in a better place until we ground our policies on a better understanding of China and adopt smarter policies that work better for the American people. And when we do that, we’ll see better results and the relationship will be better.”
Firestein said the current US policy was failing because it was based on a shift in assumptions from 10 to 15 years ago that he believed was “dead wrong”.
He noted that viewing China as an enemy was a drastic change from seeing the country as a partner or even a competitor.
“From partner to enemy is the first fundamental change in assumptions that we see, and it’s a major – and I would say, radical – departure from where we were,” he said.
Washington has never labelled China as an enemy, but Trump described the country as a strategic “competitor” in his first administration and his successor, Joe Biden, followed suit, singling out China as “our most serious competitor”.
Firestein said Washington had missed another important point – that China was also “an indispensable partner to the United States”.
“Both of those things are true at the same time,” he said. “The Chinese are able to process that better than we are.”


The former diplomat noted that the move from “engagement” to “disengagement” was manifested in highly restrictive policies like decoupling and technology controls and that it served the long-term interests of the US, marking a drastic shift from its perceptions 20 to 25 years ago.
Firestein said Washington also believed that “China seeks to displace and supplant the United States as the world’s only superpower, as we the United States define the term superpower”. He said that was a radical departure from what was assumed a few years ago.
During his meeting with Trump in Busan, Xi said that “China never seeks to challenge or replace any country and it focuses on doing its own business well”, state broadcaster CGTN reported.
Xi made similar comments in his meeting with Biden in San Francisco in 2023.
According to Firestein, American policy was failing because the country was not achieving its own goals.
“It’s veering off course because the foundational assumptions that undergird the policy are wrong – and therefore, the resulting policies that stem from those strategic assumptions are misguided.”
He said anti-China sentiment also served to deflect blame for failures at home, and it served to rally voters and give politicians ammunition to attack their opponents. For that reason, he said Washington often preferred China to be a “problem unsolved” rather than a “problem solved”.

data.title: China’s sovereign fund sidelines executives amid worries over US scrutiny
data.description:
China’s flagship sovereign wealth fund has moved three of its managing directors to new, subordinate roles, sources told the Post, with the heads of the fund’s fixed income, private equity and public relations departments all taking new non-managerial positions.
All have extensive overseas work experience, and none have reached the statutory retirement age. More reorganising could be on the horizon at China Investment Corporation (CIC), the sources said on condition of anonymity, including those managing the daily operations of US portfolios.
No reasons were given for the reshuffle, and the fund did not immediately reply to an emailed request for comment.
The rapid-fire reassignment of senior staff members raises questions over the status of the fund’s overseas operations, particularly in the US, where CIC could become a target of security screenings despite a recent bilateral trade truce.
CIC, which currently manages US$1.3 trillion in assets, is perhaps best known in the United States for its equity investments in Blackstone and Morgan Stanley in its early years, and a US$5 billion cooperation fund with Goldman Sachs established in 2017.
It set up a New York representative office 10 years ago, one of only two offices for the fund outside mainland China, with the other in Hong Kong.
CIC, founded in 2007 with US$200 billion in registered capital, faces rising challenges in its American operations as relations between the two countries sour under the second term of US President Donald Trump.
The Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States, an inter-agency body that reviews overseas inflows, has enhanced its scrutiny of Chinese investments, and the White House unveiled plans for the creation of a US sovereign wealth fund in February.
“One area where the US could take action, or where tense US-China relations might affect things, is CIC’s investments in American hedge, private equity and venture capital funds,” said Jean-Marc Blanchard, founding executive director of the Wong MNC Centre, an independent think tank based in the US state of California.
We have seen funds reluctant to accept ‘Chinese’ money, and the US could press for disclosure of CIC investments
Jean-Marc Blanchard, Wong MNC Centre
The Trump administration, which has fought pitched battles with China on several economic fronts this year, has publicly considered leveraging the US’ dominant role in the global financial system to stay ahead of Beijing in their broad rivalry. If implemented, some of these measures could target China’s sovereign funds.
While a one-year truce on trade escalations, solidified after a meeting between President Xi Jinping and Trump last month, has meant a temporary quelling of open conflict in some arenas, few believe the feud between the world’s two largest economies has come to an end – and that is having a chilling effect on investment, Blanchard said.
“We have seen funds reluctant to accept ‘Chinese’ money, and the US could press for disclosure of CIC investments, which might further increase their reluctance.”
The Chinese sovereign wealth fund has yet to release its financial statement for last year, which typically includes details about its overseas investment portfolio, its returns and its exposure to the US equity market.
Since CIC began issuing its annual report in 2009, the overview has typically arrived in July or August. While the 2024 report is not yet available, the fund may have changed its publishing schedule; the editions covering 2021, 2022 and 2023 were not made public until the last three months of the subsequent years.
The fund has been cautious in 2025, according to institutions that track its overseas investments, with CIC absent from the financing plans of some of its long-time US partners.
Its report for 2023 showed that US stocks accounted for 60.3 per cent of its overseas public equity holdings that year. It directly managed 36.5 per cent of its overseas portfolio, while the rest was handled through entrusted investment.
A majority of its assets are held under its subsidiary Central Huijin Investment, a firm which acts as a shareholder and stock trader on behalf of the state. The company holds a significant or majority stake in nearly all of China’s leading financial institutions.
Under the leadership of Zhang Qingsong, a former deputy governor of China’s central bank, the CIC vowed this year to optimise the structure of outbound investment and improve its own investment capabilities.
Its midyear work statement, released in early August, continued to highlight the optimisation of asset allocation and mentioned the need for risk control in overseas investment.

data.title: Fugitive ex-Philippine official accuses Marcos of ordering bogus flood control projects
data.description:
A fugitive former Philippine lawmaker has accused President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr of directing him to add 100 billion pesos (US$1.7 billion) worth of dubious public works projects to the national budget, a claim that throws new fuel on the corruption scandal that has dogged his administration for months.
Analysts say the explosive allegation, made in a video sent to reporters by ex-appropriations chairman Zaldy Co, could feed a wider destabilisation push by rival political blocs, including allies of Vice-President Sara Duterte-Carpio who are preparing a major protest this weekend.
Co, once a key Marcos ally, resigned in late September after claiming his family faced “real, direct, grave and imminent” threats amid the flood control investigations.
“I hope I don’t get killed before I am able to reveal everything I know,” he said in the six-minute video. “[The president] told me, ‘I will shoot you if you talk.’”


Co said he left the country on July 19 for what he described as a medical check-up abroad, days before Marcos’ July 28 State of the Nation Address in which the president castigated the anomalous budget items.
“But former Speaker Martin told me to stay out of the country. You will be taken care of as instructed by the president,” Co said, referring to Marcos’ cousin and then House leader Martin Romualdez.
His claims place new scrutiny on the flood control works at the centre of a widening “ghost projects” scandal, in which billions of pesos in public works funds were allegedly channelled into contracts that were poorly documented, substandard or never built at all – fuelling public anger and multiple investigations.
The Marcos-created Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) said it would investigate the accusations.
Dave Gomez, acting secretary of the Presidential Communications Office, called the allegations “wild” and “pure hearsay”.
Gomez reminded the public it was the president who had exposed the anomalies and “taken numerous steps … to ensure the guilty are brought to justice, the stolen wealth recovered, and the system is fixed to avoid any of these from happening again”.
Co alleged that the president personally instructed him to add the disputed projects during last year’s drafting of the 2025 national budget, recounting a series of phone calls and meetings involving senior officials.
He said that sometime in December, as senators and lawmakers met to reconcile differences in the spending bill, Budget Secretary Amenah Pangandaman phoned him to say that Marcos wanted 100 billion pesos worth of projects added to the bicameral conference report.
Co claimed she told him he could verify the instruction with Executive Secretary Lucas Bersamin – whom he mistakenly referred to as “Adrian” – because Bersamin had been present at the meeting where the president discussed the matter.
Co said he called Bersamin immediately and asked whether Marcos had issued the direction.
“He told me that is true,” he said.
He said he then contacted Romualdez, who allegedly told him, “what the president wants, he gets”.

Co added that Bersamin later handed him a list of projects to be added, explaining that Marcos had pulled the list out of “his brown leather bag”.
He used the anecdote to underscore the importance the president placed on the bag, recalling a May 2022 trip to Singapore where Marcos reportedly declared that “everything could be left behind except for that brown leather bag”.
Co said he warned Pangandaman that adding the items would make the public works budget larger than the education budget – a potential violation of the 1987 constitution – but was told to proceed anyway.
“Sec Mina told me, just do it … In other words, the order of the king cannot be broken,” he said.
Co is facing his own legal troubles relating to the ghost project scandal. The ICI has recommended charges against him involving 290 million pesos worth of flood control projects in Oriental Mindoro.
Political risk analyst Ronald Llamas said Co’s allegations were “not surprising”, describing them as part of his “transactional approach” to politics.
“Sooner or later, he will have to transact – who will give him protection, who will save his fortune,” Llamas told This Week in Asia.
He said Co’s account should be “taken with a grain of salt”, arguing that it was “weird” for a president to rely on informal insertions when he already oversees the preparation of the national budget before it is submitted to Congress.
Llamas said Co needed to provide “concrete evidence” to back his claims, but warned that the allegations could nonetheless become the spark for a destabilisation bid, beginning with this Sunday’s rally by Duterte-Carpio supporters.
The protest is the latest flashpoint in a deepening power struggle between the Marcos and Duterte clans, whose alliance swept them to a joint victory in the 2022 elections but has since unravelled amid public feuds and mutual accusations of betrayal.
“This has been the trigger they are looking for,” he said, noting that Senator Imee Marcos – the president’s estranged sister and a Duterte ally – had earlier announced that Co would testify at the Senate investigation into flood control anomalies.

He warned that the risk of a withdrawal of military support for Marcos had increased because Co, as a former palace insider, could lend credibility to destabilisation narratives.
“The possibility of a destabilisation, a withdrawal of support, just got much bigger because of [Co’s] personality,” he said.
Following Co’s revelations, Armed Forces of the Philippines chief General Romeo Brawner Jnr said the military was currently verifying reports of an “active-duty soldier” being involved in destabilisation efforts.
“Those who are calling for soldiers to join the rallies and calling for a coup d’etat – don’t expect the [armed forces] to carry out unconstitutional activities,” he warned.
“We will not stage a coup. We will not become a military junta, because the country will suffer for it.”
Llamas said both China and the US were likely to involve themselves if the political crisis deepened, because a transition to a Duterte-Carpio government “will favour China, it will not be favourable for the Americans, especially if the goal is to install Sara”.
He also pointed to recent OCTA Research findings that showed 70 per cent of Filipinos opposed military intervention in politics.
“But the problem is, even a failed [military] mutiny can be as damaging as a successful one, with the economy being as it is right now and with our weakened political institutions,” he said.
A veteran financial-market figure, speaking on condition of anonymity, said he was suspicious of Co’s timing: the video was released just days before a large protest this Sunday, reportedly backed by supporters of the Die‑hard Duterte Supporters movement together with members of the Iglesia ni Cristo, the Kingdom of Jesus Christ and the Jesus Is Lord Church Worldwide – a loose alliance of political, religious and grass-roots groups.
“If Co’s revelations are true, then the US-Philippine peso currency exchange rates and the stock market will fall and the economy could suffer more,” he said.

data.title: Uber urges Hong Kong to adopt ‘flexible’ driver quota based on service metrics
data.description:
Ride-hailing giant Uber has urged the Hong Kong government to adopt a “flexible” quota regime that will allow more drivers to work for ride-hailing platforms if service quality indicators, such as waiting times, are not met.
Nicole Lee Tsz-yu, Uber Hong Kong’s head of public policy and government affairs, said on Friday that the government should consider a quota system that would allow for more drivers to be licensed in accordance with market demand to avoid residents having to wait longer or pay more for ride-hailing services due to a limited number of drivers.
“If we have a more flexible, dynamic quota system, it can really help to reflect and also adjust the market according to the rider demand,” she said.
Lee was speaking at a forum organised by the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology to discuss the city’s coming ride-hailing regulation framework and the future landscape of the industry.
The panel discussion was also attended by representatives from Grab, a Singapore-headquartered ride-hailing service platform operating across Southeast Asia, and two researchers.
Lawmakers passed the Road Traffic (Amendment) Bill last month, legalising ride-hailing operations in Hong Kong. The government is expected to propose a regulatory framework early next year, with licences to be issued in the middle of 2026.
The licensing system will set vehicle age and insurance requirements. Ride-hailing platforms, drivers and the vehicles will also be required to have separate licences and permits.
Authorities also plan to use subsidiary legislation to determine the number of licences that will be issued.
Uber’s Lee argued that it would be difficult for authorities to accurately calculate the number of drivers needed to serve the city in the initial roll-out of the quota system, since many working for the platform were part-timers.
Lee said more than 60 per cent of Uber’s drivers in Hong Kong drove less than 20 hours per week, making their driving patterns different from full-time commercial drivers, as they could be undertaking other work commitments at the same time.
“A lot of these drivers do not drive on a full-time basis, and it’s really hard to get the initial programme out right,” Lee said.

The ride-hailing giant instead suggested the government employ some service quality indicators, such as customers’ waiting times and trip completion rate.
The second metric tallies how many trips were completed against the number requested, helping to gauge whether the supply of drivers was sufficient.
Jamie Ko, Grab’s regional public affairs and policy director, said a quota for drivers would be “inefficient” for the market as drivers may not work full time, while demand could spike during ad hoc mega-events.
Ko pointed to Singapore, where the number of ride-hailing drivers on its streets was not restricted, as demand for the service could spike during less regular types of mega-events such as concerts.
“The flexibility of quota allows people who actually don’t usually want to drive at all, but realise there’s a lot of demand and [they] probably can make some money to come out [and drive],” she said.
Professor Sylvia He Ying, an urban studies specialist at the Chinese University of Hong Kong, said that traffic could become congested as more drivers took on ride-hailing gigs.
The academic said a good understanding of the potential number or percentage of ride-hailing drivers in the city was important in determining how many quota spaces should be allowed.
Terry Chan Ka-nang, executive director for land supply or municipal traffic and transport planning with consultancy firm AECOM, said he was not worried about ride-hailing services driving up traffic congestion, as they were unlikely to convert many public transport users.
“I don’t think ride-hailing services will be as cheap as buses or anything else,” he said, noting they were “totally different markets”.
The engineer added that the substantial cost of owning and operating a car in Hong Kong meant the legalisation was unlikely to result in a surge of people buying vehicles.

data.title: Hong Kong police record more than 40 cases of online romance scams in 2 weeks
data.description:
Hong Kong police have recorded more than 40 cases of online romance scams in the past two weeks, including one of a 52-year-old woman who lost over HK$2.6 million (US$334,500) to a swindler posing as a “Singaporean businessman”.
The force on Friday said the cases involved losses totalling over HK$17 million.
It added that more than 40 per cent of the victims were aged over 50.
In a post on its CyberDefender Facebook page, the force said the 52-year-old woman met a man claiming to be a businessman from Singapore on WhatsApp.
After chatting briefly through the app, they developed what the woman believed to be an intimate relationship.
The scammer soon lured her into investing in a purported hotel project, promising high returns.
The victim trusted him fully, making 30 transfers totalling HK$2.6 million to the scammer’s designated account over a month before he disappeared.
The force last month also revealed another case in which an 81-year-old woman lost more than HK$5 million after falling victim to an elaborate online romance scam involving a fraudster posing as a People’s Liberation Army officer from mainland China.
“Falling for a scammer is like digging your own grave,” the force warned.
It reminded members of the public to be wary of online friendships, especially when expressions of affection shifted towards discussions about money.
“A fantasy romance scam is specifically designed to lure those looking for love, offering a fake virtual relationship that demands nothing but payments, with zero genuine affection.”

data.title: Stranded Chinese astronauts return, flying power plant deployed: SCMP daily highlights
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China stories of the day. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Stranded Chinese astronauts return home from space station mission
Three astronauts whose return to Earth from China’s Tiangong space station was delayed last week after suspected space debris struck their vessel have landed at the Dongfeng Landing Site in the Inner Mongolia autonomous region.
2. China’s growth softens in October as property drags investment further down
China’s economic indicators showed fading growth momentum in October, underscoring persistent domestic pressures as investment cooled despite steady consumer spending while markets grappled with uncertainty from the trade war.
3. How Chinese scientists turned a giant kite into a flying power plant

China has successfully deployed the world’s largest power-generating kite, capable of harnessing the potential of energy-dense, high-altitude winds to provide cheaper power than traditional surface turbines.
4. Xi welcomes Thai king to China with pledge of progress for family-strength ties
China stands ready to align development strategies with Thailand for “greater progress” in bilateral ties, President Xi Jinping told the visiting Thai monarch King Maha Vajiralongkorn in Beijing on Friday.
5. China’s Yangtze River Delta aids private sector, reins in ‘profit-driven’ fines

Entrepreneurs and legal experts have welcomed a move by Shanghai and its neighbouring provinces of Jiangsu, Zhejiang and Anhui to regulate cross-regional market enforcement, saying it will offer relief from arbitrary inspections, overlapping investigations and profit-driven law enforcement.
6. How Xi’s ongoing battle against PLA corruption shapes China’s military future
Even for the anti-graft era of President Xi Jinping, the wave of investigations into alleged corruption in the Chinese military over the past three years has been exceptional.
7. China’s first 076 ‘drone carrier’ amphibious assault ship begins sea trials

China’s first drone-carrying Type 076 amphibious assault ship, the Sichuan, began sea trials on Friday, after its launch in December last year, according to state news agency Xinhua.

data.title: Chinese humanoid robot makers seize the spotlight at Shenzhen’s annual hi-tech expo
data.description:
China’s humanoid robot manufacturers on Friday took centre stage at the opening of the 27th China Hi-Tech Fair in Shenzhen, as the demand for these machines in enterprises gathers speed.
The fair, which will run through Sunday, has put the spotlight this year on artificial intelligence and robotics innovation.
With about a dozen of its products on display at this year’s event, Unitree Robotics drew one of the largest crowds on opening day, as it featured humanoid robots dancing and engaging in kickboxing matches.
Other robotics exhibitors that were well received at the show included EngineAI, Cyborg and Shenzhen Ruiyan.

The fair’s spotlight on China’s robotics industry reflected the efforts by China’s public and private sectors to foster its development via supportive national policies and unique events such as the inaugural World Humanoid Robot Games held in Beijing in August.
The government has helped supercharge demand for humanoid robots in state-owned enterprises and factories, as a number of manufacturers entered mass production this year.
Shenzhen-based EngineAI showcased its star product, PM01, at the fair. The humanoid made global headlines earlier this year by performing the world’s first frontflip by a robot.
PMO1 has advanced beyond such stunts to become involved in real-world applications such as security inspection and serving as a guide at exhibitions and in tourism, according to EngineAI marketing manager Yao Weijia.
“We’re working to quickly expand the deployment scenarios based on our current applications, making it possible for humanoid robots to become ubiquitous in households,” Yao said at the fair.

Another Shenzhen start-up, Cyborg, introduced its wheel-legged humanoid robot, W01, for the factory floor. Its portfolio includes bipedal robots and dexterous robotic hands.
The expansion from bipedal to wheeled robots was a “strategic choice based on the urgent demand in industrial scenarios and the acceleration of commercialisation”, said Dong Dianbiao, CEO at Cyborg.
The W01’s wheels enable the robot to manoeuvre more efficiently in a factory, while its humanlike upper body can lift objects weighing more than 15 kilograms and perform other complicated tasks, according to the company.
“In the complex environment inside factories and warehouses, wheeled robots provide notable advantages in terms of efficiency of movement, stability and cost,” Dong said.

While Cyborg and other start-ups in the industry seek to expand the range of humanoid robots in the market, Shenzhen Ruiyan said it remained focused on building dexterous robotic hands, which are engineered to mimic how human hands perform specific tasks such as a precision grip – grasping with the tips of the thumb and index finger on either side of an object.
“We focus on dexterous hands because it’s the most complex and difficult part in a humanoid robot,” said Feng Rilin, sales director at Ruiyan. The company’s robotic hands, which cost from 10,000 yuan (US$1,400) to 80,000 yuan, are also supplied to client humanoid robot makers.
Dexterous hands were identified as one of the key areas of robotics that the Shenzhen municipal government supports, according to a range of policies announced in March.

data.title: Nexperia China slams chipmaker’s head office as Sino-Dutch envoys prepare for talks
data.description:
The Sino-Dutch row over the control of the Chinese-owned, Netherlands-based chipmaker Nexperia deepened on Friday, as the company’s China units accused the head office of “malicious sabotage” and “shirking responsibilities”.
This latest development threatened to put at risk a meeting between the Dutch and Chinese sides early next week in Beijing. The Netherlands’ Minister of Economic Affairs, Vincent Karremans, on Thursday said the dialogue was aimed at finding a “mutually agreeable solution” to the dispute.
In a joint letter sent on Friday by Nexperia’s nine units on the mainland to all employees of the Dutch chipmaker, they slammed senior management for “maliciously sabotaging Nexperia China’s production and operations” by suspending wafer supplies, casting doubt on the quality of its products and failing to provide agreed-upon funding and support.
The Dutch government “has yet to take any concrete action to cease infringing upon the lawful rights and interests of Chinese enterprises or to restore stability to the global semiconductor supply chain”, according to the letter.
The letter was apparently in response to a letter that the Dutch-installed Nexperia CEO, Stefan Tilger, sent to the chipmaker’s China employees on November 12.

The content of Tilger’s letter was not disclosed, but the Chinese team has said that it “distorted the facts and attempted to confuse the issue, which fully reflected the attitude of certain members of the management team at Nexperia Netherlands, who are shirking responsibility and disregarding the vital interests of all employees at Nexperia China”.
A Nexperia spokesman said on Friday that the letter was internal and could not be shared.
Nexperia China, which runs the chipmaker’s largest assembly and testing plant in Dongguan, southern Guangdong province, said in Friday’s letter that it was “actively pursuing production self-rescue” and strived “to maintain the orderly development of all production and business operations, while cooperating with the Chinese government’s requirements to resume exports for civilian purposes”.
After the latest round of US-China trade talks, Beijing agreed to exempt compliant orders for chips produced at Nexperia’s China facilities from export bans. Washington also suspended its new Entity List rules for a year.
German auto suppliers told the Post this week that the first shipments of Nexperia chips were already on their way to Europe after Beijing granted those exemptions.
Nexperia supplies chips to nearly every major European carmaker, as well as those from Japan and the US. It supplies so-called legacy chips used for basic applications such as power windows, lights, sensors and airbags.
The Netherlands seized control of Nexperia on September 30, citing national security concerns and invoking an obscure 1952 law known as the Goods Availability Act.
That action came a day after the US government extended export control restrictions to entities at least 50 per cent owned by companies on Washington’s trade blacklist. Being wholly owned by blacklisted Wingtech Technology, Nexperia became subject to US sanctions.
In response, Beijing imposed export controls on products from the Dongguan factory, while Nexperia’s China unit publicly rejected directives from the Dutch head office after declaring to employees and customers that it would continue operating independently.

data.title: Hamas tightens grip on Gaza as Trump plan faces hurdles
data.description:
From regulating the price of chicken to levying fees on cigarettes, Hamas is seeking to widen control over Gaza as US plans for its future slowly take shape, Gazans say, adding to rivals’ doubts over whether it will cede authority as promised.
After a ceasefire began last month, Hamas swiftly reestablished its hold over areas from which Israel withdrew, killing dozens of Palestinians it accused of collaborating with Israel, theft or other crimes. Foreign powers demand the group disarm and leave government but have yet to agree who will replace them.
Now, a dozen Gazans say they are increasingly feeling Hamas control in other ways. Authorities monitor everything coming into areas of Gaza held by Hamas, levying fees on some privately imported goods including fuel as well as cigarettes and fining merchants seen to be overcharging for goods, according to 10 of the Gazans, three of them merchants with direct knowledge.
Ismail Al-Thawabta, head of the media office of the Hamas government, said accounts of Hamas taxing cigarettes and fuel were inaccurate, denying the government was raising any taxes.
The authorities were only carrying out urgent humanitarian and administrative tasks whilst making “strenuous efforts” to control prices, Thawabta said. He reiterated Hamas’ readiness to hand over to a new technocratic administration, saying it aimed to avoid chaos in Gaza: “Our goal is for the transition to proceed smoothly.”
Hatem Abu Dalal, owner of a Gaza mall, said prices were high because not enough goods were coming into Gaza. Government representatives were trying to bring order to the economy – touring around, checking goods and setting prices, he said.
Mohammed Khalifa, shopping in central Gaza’s Nuseirat area, said prices were constantly changing despite attempts to regulate them. “It’s like a stock exchange,” he said.
“The prices are high. There’s no income, circumstances are difficult, life is hard, and winter is coming,” he said.
US President Donald Trump’s Gaza plan secured a ceasefire on October 10 and the release of the last living hostages seized during the Hamas-led October 7, 2023 attacks on Israel.


The plan calls for the establishment of a transitional authority, the deployment of a multinational security force, Hamas’ disarmament, and the start of reconstruction.
But Reuters, citing multiple sources, reported this week that Gaza’s de facto partition appeared increasingly likely, with Israeli forces still deployed in more than half the territory and efforts to advance the plan faltering.
Nearly all of Gaza’s 2 million people live in areas controlled by Hamas, which seized control of the territory from President Mahmoud Abbas’ Palestinian Authority (PA) and his Fatah Movement in 2007.
Ghaith al-Omari, a senior fellow at the Washington Institute think-tank, said Hamas’ actions aimed to show Gazans and foreign powers alike that it cannot be bypassed.
“The longer that the international community waits, the more entrenched Hamas becomes,” Omari said.

US says Hamas ‘will not govern’
Asked for comment on Gazans’ accounts of Hamas levying fees on some goods, among other reported activities, a US State Department spokesperson said: “This is why Hamas cannot and will not govern in Gaza.”
A new Gaza government can be formed once the United Nations approves Trump’s plan, the spokesperson said, adding that progress has been made towards forming the multinational force.
The PA is pressing for a say in Gaza’s new government, though Israel rejects the idea of it running Gaza again. Fatah and Hamas are at odds over how the new governing body should be formed.
Munther al-Hayek, a Fatah spokesperson in Gaza, said Hamas actions “give a clear indication that Hamas wants to continue to govern”.
In the areas held by Israel, small Palestinian groups that oppose Hamas have a foothold, a lingering challenge to it.
Gazans continue to endure dire conditions, though more aid has entered since the ceasefire.

They see and record everything
A senior Gazan food importer said Hamas hadn’t returned to a full taxation policy, but they “see and record everything”.
They monitor everything that enters, with checkpoints along routes, and stop trucks and question drivers, he said, declining to be identified. Price manipulators are fined, which helps reduce some prices, but they are still much higher than before the war began and people complain they have no money.
Hamas’ Gaza government employed up to 50,000 people, including policemen, before the war. Thawabta said that thousands of them were killed, and those remaining were ready to continue working under a new administration.
Hamas authorities continued paying them salaries during the war, though it cut the highest, standardising wages to 1,500 shekels (US$470) a month, Hamas sources and economists familiar with the matter said. It is believed that Hamas drew on stockpiled cash to pay the wages, a diplomat said.
The Hamas government replaced four regional governors who were killed, sources close to Hamas said. A Hamas official said the group also replaced 11 members of its Gaza politburo who died.
Gaza City activist and commentator Mustafa Ibrahim said Hamas was exploiting delays in the Trump plan “to bolster its rule”. “Will it be allowed to continue doing so? I think it will continue until an alternative government is in place,” he said.

data.title: Banker’s family sues clinic of Hong Kong plastic surgeon, 93, over deadly jabs
data.description:
The family of a top banker who died after receiving botox injections seven years ago has sued the Hong Kong clinic that offered the treatment, extending its founder’s legal woes just as a court earlier discharged him from a manslaughter trial.
Franklin WP Li’s Surgery Limited, which operates the Tsim Sha Tsui clinic, has been accused of negligence and breach of duty of care over the use of a combination of sedatives and anaesthetics on Zoe Cheung Shuk-ling on November 11, 2018, according to a personal injury claim initiated in the High Court last Friday.
The writ, filed by Cheung’s estate managers, Roni Cheung Mei-ling and Cheung Oi-ling, also accused plastic surgeon Dr Franklin Li Wang-pong of conspiring with his wife and son to cause “culpable delay in summoning for emergency service to convey the deceased to hospital in a timeous fashion for life-saving treatment”.
The court document did not specify the amount of compensation sought. The first hearing has been tentatively scheduled for late April next year.
Cheung, who was the managing director of Swiss private bank Julius Baer in the city, died aged 52 on November 12, 2018, after sustaining hypoxic brain damage from oversedation.
Earlier this year, a High Court jury ruled that Li, 93, was unfit to stand trial for manslaughter due to his advanced dementia.
But the seven-strong panel nonetheless returned a unanimous decision that the doctor, who was 86 at the time of the incident, unlawfully killed the banker due to gross negligence, including his use of potentially lethal drugs on Cheung and concealment of critical information from police and hospital staff.
The trial also revealed that Li, his wife Veronica Li Lau Wei-chue, and their son Dr Konrad Li Ming-sheng, did not immediately call an ambulance after Cheung fell into a coma, as they attempted to resuscitate her at the clinic using a bag valve device.
Franklin Li is expected to appear in Kowloon City Court in January to face charges of misleading police and failing to maintain a register for dangerous drugs.
The late banker’s family has previously sued Li and his family in separate civil proceedings.
